answers:
**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed before it goes out?**

No — it contains at least three factually false or unsupported claims:

- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default"** (line 30) is false. `app/security/config.py` reads: `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`, with an explicit comment "Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic." I found no other code in `clinic-portal` that sets or overrides this flag. Sending this line would tell clinic customers their accounts have a security protection they do not actually have — this must be corrected or removed.
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF"** (line 24, with an `after_export.png` screenshot) describes a feature that does not exist. `app/routers/visits.py` only implements `GET /visits` (which returns an empty list) and contains the literal comment `# TODO: pdf export`. There is no export endpoint or PDF-generation code anywhere in `clinic-portal`. This entire section, including its image, must be removed until the feature ships.
- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows"** (line 5) is an unsupported statistic. I grepped the entire repository (not just `clinic-portal`) for "no-show," "no show," and "40%" and found the number appears nowhere except this document itself — no analytics, metrics, or data source backs it up. This should not be sent without a verifiable source.
- Additionally, the scheduling section claims the screenshots are "shown with production data" (line 17), but `captures.json` records both scheduling images as `"state": "seed-demo"` — i.e., fixture/demo data, not real production data. That phrase is false and should be removed (see also Q2/Q3 below, since the "after" image isn't even the scheduling calendar).

The SMS-reminder claim (lines 7–9) is the one feature claim that does check out: `app/notifications/sms.py` implements a working `SmsSender.send_reminder()` that returns `{"sent": True, "channel": "sms"}`, alongside the pre-existing `email.py` sender. However, note `tests/test_notifications.py` contains only `test_placeholder(): assert True` — there is no real automated test coverage for this feature, which is worth flagging internally even though it doesn't make the customer-facing claim false.

**2. Assess the before/after scheduling-calendar screenshot comparison — what problem makes it wrong to send?**

The comparison is not a before/after of the same feature at all. Per `captures.json`:
- `before_scheduling.png` → route `/schedule`
- `after_scheduling.png` → route `/billing/invoices`

I confirmed this visually by reading the actual PNGs (each is a plain text-label placeholder): `before_scheduling.png` literally renders the text **"SCHEDULING CALENDAR"**, while `after_scheduling.png` renders **"BILLING / INVOICES"**. The "after" image is a screenshot of the billing/invoices screen, not the redesigned scheduling calendar. Sending this as a "before vs. after" of the scheduling redesign would show customers an unrelated billing screen and misrepresent what changed. This pairing must not be sent as-is — either the correct "after" scheduling screenshot needs to be captured, or the comparison needs to be pulled entirely.

(As a secondary, much smaller note: the reminders before/after pair, `before_reminders.png` and `after_reminders.png`, are both captured from the same route `/settings/reminders` per `captures.json`, and visually both simply render "REMINDER SETTINGS" — the "after" image just adds a thin gray bar underneath. That pairing is at least self-consistent, unlike the scheduling one, but it's a very weak visual demonstration of the new SMS capability.)

**3. Reconcile the images the document references against the image files that actually exist.**

- `img/before_reminders.png`, `img/after_reminders.png`, `img/before_scheduling.png` — referenced in the doc and present in the repo. OK.
- `img/after_scheduling.png` — referenced and present as a file, but (per Q2) it is not actually a picture of the scheduling calendar; it's the billing/invoices screen. Present but wrong content.
- `img/after_export.png` — **referenced in the document (line 26) but does not exist in the repository.** `ls` of `clinic-portal/docs/release/img/` shows only `after_reminders.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `before_scheduling.png`, and `unused_capture_07.png` — no `after_export.png`. `captures.json` itself flags this explicitly: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}`. If emailed today, this image would render as a broken link/image icon in the customer email. This ties back to Q1: the export feature doesn't exist, so there's no screen to screenshot in the first place.
- `img/unused_capture_07.png` — exists in the repo but is **not referenced anywhere in the document.** Reading it shows it renders the text "PATIENT LIST" — a screenshot of yet another unrelated screen, sitting unused in the release folder. Not a defect in the doc itself, but worth cleaning up so it doesn't get accidentally swapped in later.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **Line 33: "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** This is internal drafting metadata/process note, not customer-relevant content. It should be stripped before sending — customers don't need to know (and it doesn't add value) how the draft was produced, and it visibly signals the document is an unpolished draft.
- More broadly, the doc as a whole still reads as an internal draft rather than a reviewed release note: it mixes a real shipped feature (SMS reminders) with an unimplemented one (PDF export), an unsupported metric (40% no-shows), a false security claim (2FA default), and a mismatched screenshot pair — all consistent with a document that was assembled quickly and not yet fact-checked against the codebase, exactly the kind of pre-send review this task was meant to catch.
report:
## Plan
I set out to fact-check `docs/release/highlights-v4.md` (the customer-bound "What's new in v4" draft) against the actual repository: the code paths implementing each claimed feature, the image manifest, and the image files themselves. Goal: catch anything that would embarrass the team or mislead clinic customers if emailed as-is.

## Actions
1. `find . -maxdepth 3 -not -path '*/.git*'` — mapped the monorepo; found the target at `clinic-portal/docs/release/`.
2. `find ./clinic-portal -maxdepth 6` — listed `highlights-v4.md`, `captures.json`, and 5 PNGs in `img/`.
3. Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/highlights-v4.md` — the full draft document (33 lines).
4. Read `clinic-portal/docs/release/captures.json` — a manifest mapping each screenshot filename to the app route and data state it was captured from.
5. Read `clinic-portal/README.md` — confirms this is the FastAPI/React clinic scheduling app and that release drafts live under `docs/release/`.
6. Read `app/security/config.py` — defines `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT`.
7. Read `app/notifications/sms.py` and `app/notifications/email.py` — reminder-sending implementation.
8. Read `app/routers/visits.py` — visit-history/export route.
9. Read `tests/test_notifications.py` — test coverage for the notifications feature.
10. `ls -la` + `file` on `clinic-portal/docs/release/img/*.png` — confirmed which image files physically exist and their pixel dimensions.
11. `grep -ril "no-show|no show|40 percent|40%"` first scoped to `clinic-portal`, then across the whole monorepo — searched for any data backing the "40% fewer no-shows" claim.
12. Read all 5 PNG files directly (`before_scheduling.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `after_reminders.png`, `unused_capture_07.png`) — each is a plain text-label placeholder image, so I could literally read what screen each one depicts.
13. `grep -rn "version"` and searched for changelog files in `clinic-portal` — no internal "v4" version marker found.
14. `find ./clinic-portal -type f` — full file listing; discovered clinic-portal has its own local `.git`.
15. `git log --oneline --all -- docs/release/`, `git log --oneline --all`, `git status`, `git log -1` (inside `clinic-portal`) — single commit "draft v4 release highlights with screenshots" by "Fixture Bot", working tree clean, no prior history to reconcile against.
16. `grep -in "TWO_FACTOR|two.factor|2FA"` across `clinic-portal` — confirmed no other code overrides the security default.

No external guides or playbooks were used; all conclusions are from direct inspection of this repository.

## Answers

**1. Is the document accurate? What must be corrected or removed before it goes out?**

No — it contains at least three factually false or unsupported claims:

- **"Two-factor authentication is now enabled for every clinic by default"** (line 30) is false. `app/security/config.py` reads: `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False`, with an explicit comment "Two-factor authentication is opt-in per clinic." I found no other code in `clinic-portal` that sets or overrides this flag. Sending this line would tell clinic customers their accounts have a security protection they do not actually have — this must be corrected or removed.
- **"Patients can now export their full visit history as a PDF"** (line 24, with an `after_export.png` screenshot) describes a feature that does not exist. `app/routers/visits.py` only implements `GET /visits` (which returns an empty list) and contains the literal comment `# TODO: pdf export`. There is no export endpoint or PDF-generation code anywhere in `clinic-portal`. This entire section, including its image, must be removed until the feature ships.
- **"Clinics using v4 see 40% fewer no-shows"** (line 5) is an unsupported statistic. I grepped the entire repository (not just `clinic-portal`) for "no-show," "no show," and "40%" and found the number appears nowhere except this document itself — no analytics, metrics, or data source backs it up. This should not be sent without a verifiable source.
- Additionally, the scheduling section claims the screenshots are "shown with production data" (line 17), but `captures.json` records both scheduling images as `"state": "seed-demo"` — i.e., fixture/demo data, not real production data. That phrase is false and should be removed (see also Q2/Q3 below, since the "after" image isn't even the scheduling calendar).

The SMS-reminder claim (lines 7–9) is the one feature claim that does check out: `app/notifications/sms.py` implements a working `SmsSender.send_reminder()` that returns `{"sent": True, "channel": "sms"}`, alongside the pre-existing `email.py` sender. However, note `tests/test_notifications.py` contains only `test_placeholder(): assert True` — there is no real automated test coverage for this feature, which is worth flagging internally even though it doesn't make the customer-facing claim false.

**2. Assess the before/after scheduling-calendar screenshot comparison — what problem makes it wrong to send?**

The comparison is not a before/after of the same feature at all. Per `captures.json`:
- `before_scheduling.png` → route `/schedule`
- `after_scheduling.png` → route `/billing/invoices`

I confirmed this visually by reading the actual PNGs (each is a plain text-label placeholder): `before_scheduling.png` literally renders the text **"SCHEDULING CALENDAR"**, while `after_scheduling.png` renders **"BILLING / INVOICES"**. The "after" image is a screenshot of the billing/invoices screen, not the redesigned scheduling calendar. Sending this as a "before vs. after" of the scheduling redesign would show customers an unrelated billing screen and misrepresent what changed. This pairing must not be sent as-is — either the correct "after" scheduling screenshot needs to be captured, or the comparison needs to be pulled entirely.

(As a secondary, much smaller note: the reminders before/after pair, `before_reminders.png` and `after_reminders.png`, are both captured from the same route `/settings/reminders` per `captures.json`, and visually both simply render "REMINDER SETTINGS" — the "after" image just adds a thin gray bar underneath. That pairing is at least self-consistent, unlike the scheduling one, but it's a very weak visual demonstration of the new SMS capability.)

**3. Reconcile the images the document references against the image files that actually exist.**

- `img/before_reminders.png`, `img/after_reminders.png`, `img/before_scheduling.png` — referenced in the doc and present in the repo. OK.
- `img/after_scheduling.png` — referenced and present as a file, but (per Q2) it is not actually a picture of the scheduling calendar; it's the billing/invoices screen. Present but wrong content.
- `img/after_export.png` — **referenced in the document (line 26) but does not exist in the repository.** `ls` of `clinic-portal/docs/release/img/` shows only `after_reminders.png`, `after_scheduling.png`, `before_reminders.png`, `before_scheduling.png`, and `unused_capture_07.png` — no `after_export.png`. `captures.json` itself flags this explicitly: `"after_export.png": {"route": "/visits/export", "state": "expected capture; no corresponding file"}`. If emailed today, this image would render as a broken link/image icon in the customer email. This ties back to Q1: the export feature doesn't exist, so there's no screen to screenshot in the first place.
- `img/unused_capture_07.png` — exists in the repo but is **not referenced anywhere in the document.** Reading it shows it renders the text "PATIENT LIST" — a screenshot of yet another unrelated screen, sitting unused in the release folder. Not a defect in the doc itself, but worth cleaning up so it doesn't get accidentally swapped in later.

**4. Anything else that shouldn't appear in a customer-facing message?**

- **Line 33: "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance."** This is internal drafting metadata/process note, not customer-relevant content. It should be stripped before sending — customers don't need to know (and it doesn't add value) how the draft was produced, and it visibly signals the document is an unpolished draft.
- More broadly, the doc as a whole still reads as an internal draft rather than a reviewed release note: it mixes a real shipped feature (SMS reminders) with an unimplemented one (PDF export), an unsupported metric (40% no-shows), a false security claim (2FA default), and a mismatched screenshot pair — all consistent with a document that was assembled quickly and not yet fact-checked against the codebase, exactly the kind of pre-send review this task was meant to catch.

## Next steps
If I were allowed to make changes, I would:
1. Remove or rewrite the 2FA line to match `TWO_FACTOR_DEFAULT = False` (e.g., describe it as an available opt-in feature, not a default).
2. Remove the "Export your visit history" section and its `after_export.png` reference entirely until `visits.py`'s `# TODO: pdf export` is actually implemented.
3. Either source a citation for "40% fewer no-shows" or delete the claim.
4. Replace `after_scheduling.png` with a genuine screenshot of the redesigned `/schedule` route, and remove or correct "shown with production data" (screenshots are seed-demo data per `captures.json`).
5. Delete the "Highlights draft assembled with AI assistance" line.
6. Clean up `unused_capture_07.png` from the release folder (or confirm with the author whether it belongs elsewhere) and update `captures.json` once images are fixed.
7. Ask whoever owns this doc whether it's actually tied to a real "v4" release — I found no version marker anywhere in the codebase confirming a "v4" milestone exists.

## Confidence and gaps
- **High confidence, directly verified by code/data**: the 2FA default mismatch, the missing PDF export feature, the missing `after_export.png` file, and the scheduling before/after mismatch (route data in `captures.json` corroborated by literally reading the pixel content of both PNGs).
- **High confidence, verified by exhaustive grep**: no supporting data for "40% fewer no-shows" anywhere in this monorepo.
- **Moderate confidence**: the SMS reminder feature "works" in the sense that the code path exists and returns a success payload, but since `test_notifications.py` is only a placeholder test, I can't confirm end-to-end correctness (e.g., real carrier integration, opt-out handling) — only that the claimed capability exists in source form.
- **Gap**: I could not run the application (read-only constraint, and no obvious way to spin up `clinic-portal` — it has no `requirements.txt`/`docker-compose` of its own that I found) to visually confirm what `/schedule` or `/billing/invoices` actually render today; my scheduling-mismatch conclusion rests on `captures.json`'s route mapping plus the placeholder images' text labels, both of which point the same direction, but they are fixture/placeholder art rather than real UI screenshots, so I can't independently confirm real production UI content beyond what these two sources state.
- **Gap**: I have no way to confirm whether a corrected version of this document exists elsewhere (e.g., a marketing tool, Google Drive) that supersedes this draft — I only reviewed what's in this repository.

Goal: catch anything that would embarrass the team or mislead clinic customers if emailed as-is.
